title: Entanglement Enabled Data Transmission over an Arbitrarily Varying Channel
abstract: Shared randomness is the central ingredient for stabilizing symmetrizable communication systems against arbitrarily varying jammers. Given the presence of the jammer, however, the question arises how this precious resource could have been distributed. Several works discuss the use of external sources for this task. In this work, we show, based on the most standard optical communication model, how the sender and receiver can employ entangled two-mode squeezed states to counter the jamming attack of an energy-limited jammer during the distribution phase when both the sender and jammer are allowed to use binary phase shift keying and two-mode squeezed vacuum states.
summary: The paper shows that entanglement can be used to establish shared correlations between a sender and receiver to overcome jamming attacks in optical channels. By using Two-Mode Squeezed Vacuum states, the parties can transform a zero-capacity, symmetrizable channel into a functional communication channel. This provides a way to 'bootstrap' communication in environments where an adversary is actively trying to disrupt signal synchronization.
main_problem: The paper addresses the 'bootstrap' problem in communication: how a sender and receiver can establish shared randomness to stabilize an Arbitrarily Varying Channel (AVC) against a jammer when no pre-shared resources exist.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that using entangled Two-Mode Squeezed Vacuum (TMSV) states allows the sender and receiver to break channel symmetrizability and achieve positive communication capacity, even when a jammer can otherwise reduce the capacity to zero.
method: The study uses Gaussian covariance matrices, Wigner functions, and information-theoretic bounds (like Pinsker's inequality) to analyze a correlation distribution procedure involving homodyne detection and sign binarization.
model_or_system: A quantum-optical communication model using a beam splitter with transmissivity eta, involving coherent states for BPSK signaling and Two-Mode Squeezed Vacuum (TMSV) states as a quantum resource, subject to an energy-limited Gaussian jammer.
key_observables: Binarized correlation coefficient (rho_bin), quadrature measurements (x, p), and channel capacities (C, C_bar, C_tilde).
important_parameters: Squeezing magnitude (r), beam splitter transmissivity (eta), jamming energy (alpha^2), and BPSK amplitude (alpha).
main_assumptions: The jammer is assumed to be energy-limited and restricted to using Gaussian states; the communication model is based on a standard optical setup.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a visualization of the probability space of correlations, depicting the set of possible correlations within a convex polytope.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the jamming problem and the bootstrap challenge, describes the quantum-optical communication model and TMSV protocol, presents the mathematical proof for capacity enhancement and correlation persistence, and concludes with an appendix proving the monotonicity of binarized Gaussian correlations.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it explores how entanglement-assisted protocols can provide resilience against adversarial noise (jamming) and demonstrates a super-activation-like phenomenon in bosonic channels.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Depicted are O and ∆as part of the convex polytope P({0, 1}2), using r = A = 2. We observe that O approaches q0 and q1 in its limits.
